text.title: JPA/Hibernate: Entity Mapping Rules
text.summary: Rules for @Entity definitions: auditing timestamps, fetch strategy justification, cascade declaration, @Version for optimistic locking, and required JPA annotations.

rule.statement: Annotate created/updated timestamps with @CreationTimestamp and @UpdateTimestamp (Hibernate-specific) or use @EntityListeners(AuditingEntityListener.class) with @CreatedDate / @LastModifiedDate (Spring Data JPA). Mark createdAt with updatable=false.
rule.rationale: Manual timestamp management in service code is error-prone and misses bulk update paths. Hibernate-managed timestamps are applied at the persistence layer regardless of which code path modifies the entity.

rule.statement: Every @OneToMany and @ManyToMany must carry an explicit justification comment for cascade and orphanRemoval choices. Never use CascadeType.ALL without a written reason. FetchType.LAZY is the default and the correct default for collections.
rule.rationale: CascadeType.ALL with orphanRemoval=true on a relation to an entity referenced elsewhere corrupts data — orphanRemoval deletes the child when it is removed from the parent collection, even if other entities still reference it.

rule.statement: Add @Version on user-editable aggregates to enable optimistic locking. Use LockModeType.PESSIMISTIC_WRITE on repository methods for inventory / balance writes where lost updates must be prevented.
rule.rationale: Without locking, two concurrent requests that both read and then update the same row produce a lost update: the second write silently overwrites the first without either party knowing.

text.reference: Entity skeleton with auditing, lazy relations, @Version
Safe JPA defaults: open-in-view=false, ddl-auto=validate, batch inserts
